planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Big Blue is the most widely planted clumping liriope cultivar and establishes readily in a very wide range of conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Big Blue is the most commonly grown clumping liriope cultivar — a robust, grass-like perennial ground cover forming dense clumps of arching, dark-green, strap-like leaves about one-half inch wide, topped by erect spikes of dark violet-purple flowers in late summer followed by shiny black berries in fall. It is the workhorse of shade ground covers across much of the South and Gulf Coast, performing reliably under trees, on slopes, and in any location where turf grass fails due to insufficient light. Big Blue is notably more vigorous than the species and maintains its dark color better through the extremes of Gulf Coast summers. The clumping (non-spreading) habit means it stays where planted without becoming invasive, unlike the running lilyturf species. In zone 9, liriope stays green year-round as a fully evergreen ground cover. Mow or cut back to 3–4 inches in late winter (January–February in zone 9) before new growth emerges to remove worn winter foliage and stimulate a fresh flush of clean leaves. Excellent under live oaks, pines, and other established trees where few ground covers survive.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Variegata performs best in partial shade — the cream-yellow leaf margins may bleach or scorch in full hot afternoon sun, especially in zone 9 summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Variegated Lilyturf is a widely grown selection of clumping liriope distinguished by its creamy-yellow to cream-white leaf margins that contrast brightly with the dark-green central stripe — providing far more visual interest in shaded situations than the all-green types. In the shade garden, the yellow-variegated foliage acts as a natural light reflector, brightening dark areas and creating the appearance of filtered light even in deep shade. The lavender flower spikes in late summer are produced reliably through the variegated foliage and add seasonal interest. In zone 9, the foliage remains evergreen year-round but benefits from being cut back to 3–4 inches in late January or early February before new growth begins to maintain a fresh, clean appearance. The variegated form is somewhat slower-growing than Big Blue but reaches the same density with patience. Use as edging along shaded beds, in sweeping masses under trees, or as a foliage accent in shade container plantings. Deer typically avoid established liriope once it is recognized as unpalatable.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Snowflake blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after bloom if needed, never in fall or winter.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Snowflake oakleaf hydrangea is a superior selection of the native oakleaf hydrangea, distinguished by its remarkable double flowers — each individual floret in the large panicle cluster bears extra petals and sepals that give the entire flower head a dense, fully doubled appearance unlike the single-flowered species. The enormous, conical flower clusters can reach 12 inches long and are an extraordinary display in late spring and early summer in zone 9 (May–June). The flowers open pure white, age to soft rose-pink over several weeks, then become a papery tan-cream that persists through fall as attractive dried flower heads. Snowflake was selected and patented by plantsman Jeff Beasley of Piccadilly Farm in Georgia. The large, deeply lobed, oak-like leaves develop outstanding burgundy-red to reddish-purple fall color in September–October in zone 9 — exceptional for a shade shrub. The attractive exfoliating cinnamon-brown bark provides winter interest after leaf drop. Snowflake grows somewhat larger than the single-flowered species but retains the same outstanding adaptability to humid southeastern conditions.

common_name: Pee Wee Oakleaf Hydrangea (Dwarf Oakleaf Hydrangea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Pee Wee is the most compact oakleaf hydrangea cultivar widely available and provides all the species' ornamental attributes in a much smaller package appropriate for foundation plantings and small gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Pee Wee is a compact dwarf selection of the native oakleaf hydrangea that stays only 2.5–4 feet tall and wide — dramatically smaller than the standard species that can reach 6–8 feet or more — making it appropriate for foundation plantings, smaller shade gardens, and situations where the full-size species would be overwhelming. Despite the smaller size, Pee Wee provides all the ornamental attributes that make oakleaf hydrangea outstanding: conical white flower clusters in late spring that age through pink to papery tan; deeply lobed, large, textured foliage that turns rich burgundy-red in fall in zone 9; and attractive exfoliating cinnamon-brown bark that provides winter interest. The flowers are proportionally smaller than the species but still conspicuous and attractive. Pee Wee is one of the few native shade shrubs that truly provides four-season interest in the Gulf Coast garden. Like all oakleaf hydrangeas, it blooms on old wood — never prune in fall or winter, trim only immediately after the main bloom period if shaping is needed. Pee Wee is outstanding for shaded rain gardens and areas with seasonal moisture variation.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Alice is a large-growing cultivar — allow 6–8 feet in all directions at planting time to avoid future overcrowding.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Alice oakleaf hydrangea is an outstanding cultivar selected and named for Alice Recknagel Ireys, a distinguished American landscape architect, by Michael Dirr at the University of Georgia — the plant's finest characteristics reflecting the horticultural standards of its namesake. Alice produces exceptionally large, upright panicle flower clusters — sometimes reaching 14 inches long — that are among the biggest of any oakleaf hydrangea cultivar, creating a spectacular mid-May to June display in zone 9 gardens. The individual flowers open pure white and age gradually to a beautiful soft rose-pink, creating clusters that simultaneously show white and pink tones. The foliage is large, bold, and deeply lobed with the characteristic oak-leaf silhouette, turning rich burgundy-red in autumn in zone 9. Alice grows vigorously to 6–10 feet tall and equally wide, making it an outstanding large-scale specimen, naturalizing under trees, or massing for a bold woodland effect. The peeling cinnamon-brown bark is ornamental in winter. Alice received the Award of Garden Merit from the Royal Horticultural Society, confirming its outstanding ornamental performance.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society Award of Garden Merit

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Evergreen Giant is more heat- and sun-tolerant than most liriopes and can be used in brighter positions than smaller cultivars while still performing well in shade.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Evergreen Giant is a distinctively large-growing liriope cultivar that reaches 18–24 inches tall — nearly twice the height of standard Big Blue — with correspondingly wide, bold, arching leaves that give it a dramatically different presence in the landscape. Where standard liriope reads as a fine-textured edge plant or ground cover, Evergreen Giant has the bold, architectural quality of a small ornamental grass and can serve as a substitute for clumping ornamental grasses in shade or semi-shade situations where grasses typically fail. The white to pale lavender flower spikes are produced reliably above the foliage in late summer. Evergreen Giant is particularly well-adapted to zone 9 heat and humidity, maintaining better summer appearance than some of the smaller liriope cultivars that can look worn by August in the Deep South. It is also somewhat more drought-tolerant than smaller cultivars once established, performing adequately in drier spots under established tree canopies. Cut back to 4–6 inches in late winter (January in zone 9) before new growth begins to refresh the foliage. Outstanding mass-planted as a bold, low-maintenance ground cover under trees, along driveways, or on slopes.
